texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Post-combustion capture (PCC) is one of the most promising technologies to capture CO2 from power plants. New amine based solvents for that purpose are presently developed with the aim of reducing the energy penalty (Mitchell, 2008; Freeman et al., 2010; Spuhl et al., 2011; Knudsen et al., 2011; Mangalapally and Hasse, 2011b; Mangalapally et al., 2012).
texts.text: Post-combustion capture (PCC) is one of the most promising technologies to capture CO2 from power plants. New amine based solvents for that purpose are presently developed with the aim of reducing the energy penalty (Mitchell, 2008; Freeman et al., 2010; Spuhl et al., 2011; Knudsen et al., 2011; Mangalapally and Hasse, 2011b; Mangalapally et al., 2012).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The present paper reports on testing two new amine based solvents in a pilot plant. The method that was applied for the studies is the one recently introduced by Mangalapally and Hasse (2011b) (see also Mangalapally et al., 2012). Two basic sets of experiments are carried out: the variation of L / G ratio and the variation of flue gas flow rate. For the variation of L / G ratio, the solvent flow rate ( L ) is varied at a constant flue gas flow rate ( G ) (constant F -factor) and a constant CO2 partial pressure in the flue gas. texts.text: a b s t r a c t

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Pilot plant experiments for post-combustion capture (PCC) were carried out with two new amine based solvent systems: Solvent A and Solvent B. Solvent A is an aqueous solution of a secondary amine tested within an industrial partnership and Solvent B is an aqueous solution of a mixture of the sterically hindered amine 2-amino 2-methyl 1-propanol (AMP) and the primary diamine 1,2-ethanediamine (EDA) tested within the EU-project CESAR. Experiments were carried out at various liquid to gas flow ratios ( L / G ) with a constant CO2 partial pressure of 100 mbar, a constant F -factor of 1.7 Pa 0.5 and a constant CO2 removal rate of 90%, which was achieved by an adjustment of the reboiler duty. For Solvent A and B, the optimum reboiler duty was found to be reduced by 16% and 9%, respectively, compared to the standard solvent MEA. Also the numbers for the optimum L / G ratio were lower than for MEA by 37% for Solvent A and 45% for Solvent B. The influence of kinetic effects was studied by lowering the gas flow rate at a constant L / G ratio. The results show that Solvent A has similar and Solvent B slightly slower overall mass transfer kinetics compared to MEA.
texts.text: Pilot plant experiments for post-combustion capture (PCC) were carried out with two new amine based solvent systems: Solvent A and Solvent B. Experiments were carried out at various liquid to gas flow ratios ( L / G ) with a constant CO2 partial pressure of 100 mbar, a constant F -factor of 1.7 Pa 0.5 and a constant CO2 removal rate of 90%, which was achieved by an adjustment of the reboiler duty. For Solvent A and B, the optimum reboiler duty was found to be reduced by 16% and 9%, respectively, compared to the standard solvent MEA. Also the numbers for the optimum L / G ratio were lower than for MEA by 37% for Solvent A and 45% for Solvent B. The influence of kinetic effects was studied by lowering the gas flow rate at a constant L / G ratio. The results show that Solvent A has similar and Solvent B slightly slower overall mass transfer kinetics compared to MEA.

This gives a reasonable ranking of the solvents if the overall mass transfer kinetics of the solvents are similar or if the pilot plant is so amply sized that the differences in overall mass transfer kinetics do not affect the results (Mangalapally and Hasse, 2011b). For exploring the influence of the mass transfer kinetics, a variation of the flue gas flow rate is carried out at a fixed L / G ratio. Upon simultaneously decreasing both the gas and the liquid flow rate, the amount of CO2 which has to be transferred over the gas/liquid interface decreases and mass transfer limitations become less important (Mangalapally and Hasse, 2011b). As a result, a reduction of the reboiler duty with decreasing gas flow rate is obtained which is a measure of the overall mass transfer kinetics of the solvent.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The pilot plant used in the present work has a small packing height of the columns compared to industrial plants and therefore kinetic effects and heat losses play a larger role. Hence, only a relative comparison between the solvents in terms of reboiler duty and mass transfer kinetics is possible. The results for the new solvents are compared to results for the standard solvent MEA obtained in the same pilot plant. As a result, the performance of the new solvent in terms of energy consumption and mass transfer kinetics relative to MEA can be evaluated. Furthermore, information on the operability of the solvent (e.g. foaming, degradation, emissions, solid formation) is obtained. Based on the performance of a solvent in a laboratory scale pilot plant, it can be decided whether this solvent should be tested in larger pilot plants.

texts.orig: In the present work, two new solvents, Solvent A and Solvent B, were studied in a gas-fired absorption/desorption pilot plant. Their performance is compared to the standard solvent MEA and to CESAR1 and CESAR2, two solvents from the EU-project CESAR (Mangalapally and Hasse, 2011b). The composition of all solvents considered in the present work and the sources of the pilot plant data are summarized in Table 1. 'MEA' is an aqueous solution of monoethanolamine with x (m) MEA = 0 . 3 g/g and is considered as the benchmark solvent for post-combustion capture. CESAR1 is an aqueous solution of the sterically hindered amine 2amino 2-methyl 1-propanol (AMP) with x (m) AMP = 0 . 28 g/g and the secondary diamine piperazine (PIP) with x (m) PIP = 0 . 17 g/g. CESAR2 is an aqueous solution of the primary diamine 1,2-ethanediamine (EDA) with x (m) EDA = 0 . 32 g/g. Solvent A is an aqueous solution of a secondary amine with x (m) Amine = 0 . 45 g/g whose nature cannot be disclosed as it was investigated within an industrial partnership. 2 The amine is a derivative of monoethanolamine with one proton of the amine group substituted by an alkyl group. texts.label: text
texts.orig: The heat losses of the pilot plant have been analyzed in detail for the experiments with MEA by Mangalapally and Hasse (2011a) using the method described in Notz (2010) and Notz et al. (2012) which is based on energy balances using the available property data for MEA. From the experiments with MEA in our plant we know that the mean value of the heat loss is about 1.5 kW for the experiments with low CO2 partial pressure and 1.3 kW for the experiments with high CO2 partial pressure, cf. Mangalapally and Hasse (2011a,b) and Mangalapally et al. (2012), who attributed the difference to flashing. The heat loss is subtracted from the measured reboiler duty. All numbers for the reboiler duty reported in the present work are already corrected for the heat loss unless stated otherwise.

texts.orig: The quality of the liquid analytics was checked by the summation of the mass fractions of amine, water and CO2 which was between 0.98 g/g and 1.01 g/g in most cases. The absorbed CO2 mass flow can be calculated from the solvent mass flow and the measured CO2 mass fraction in the lean and rich samples and also from the gas analytics, i.e. from the difference of the gaseous CO2 mass flows at the absorber inlet and outlet. The two numbers agree within 5% or better in most cases, demonstrating both the quality of the analytics and also that steady-state was reached. In the present work, the CO2 mass flow determined from gas analytics is used for the calculation of the removal rate and the specific reboiler duty.

The removal rate of 90% was kept within a tolerance of ± 1% for most experiments except for the experiments with the lowest L / G ratio for each set, where the removal rate is typically lower (up to -9% for CESAR1). The characteristic minimum in the reboiler duty at an optimum L / G ratio can be observed for all solvents. The drastic increase to the left of the minimum is a result of the high amount of stripping steam needed to fulfill the increasingly difficult separation task in the desorber. The almost linear increase to the right of the minimum is due to the energy requirement to heat up the solvent to desorber temperature (Mangalapally et al., 2009; Mangalapally and Hasse, 2011a; Notz et al., 2011).

The almost linear increase to the right of the minimum is due to the energy requirement to heat up the solvent to desorber temperature (Mangalapally et al., 2009; Mangalapally and Hasse, 2011a; Notz et al., 2011).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The results for the optimum L / G ratios with corresponding minimum reboiler duties are summarized in Fig. 2, both in absolute numbers (a) and in a normalized format (b) (cf. Mangalapally and Hasse, 2011b). Both for Solvent A and Solvent B, the optimum L / G ratio and the corresponding minimum reboiler duty are lower compared to the standard solvent MEA. For Solvent A, the reboiler duty is 3.4 GJ/tCO 2 and, hence, only slightly higher compared to that of CESAR1 (3.2 GJ/tCO 2 ) which was the most successful solvent from the EU-project CESAR (Mangalapally and Hasse, 2011b; Knudsen et al., 2011). For Solvent B, the reboiler duty is 3.7 GJ/tCO 2 and, hence, improved over MEA (4.1 GJ/tCO 2 ) but worse than that of CESAR1. The relative improvement over MEA is 16% for Solvent A and 9% for Solvent B. The optimum L / G ratio is lower than that for MEA by 37% for Solvent A and 45% for Solvent B.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fig. 4 shows absorber operating lines for MEA, Solvent A and Solvent B at an L / G ratio of 2.5 kg/kg (optimum for MEA). The absorber operating lines are represented by straight lines connecting the CO2 partial pressure and corresponding liquid loading at the absorber bottom and top (Mangalapally and Hasse, 2011b). In principle, the operating lines can deviate strongly from a straight line. However, Mangalapally and Hasse (2011a) calculated the operating lines for the MEA experiments through mass balances over the absorber sections as suggested by Notz et al. (2012) and in fact obtained a straight line. In Fig. 4, additionally the equilibrium curves at 40 ◦ C for MEA and for Solvent B are shown. The distance between the operating line and the equilibrium curve is a measure of the driving force for mass transfer (Notz, 2010; Mangalapally et al., 2012). Solvent B needs a higher driving force for mass transfer compared to MEA, indicating lower overall mass transfer kinetics. The gas solubility data for Solvent B (Mergler, 2010) seems to overestimate the CO2 partial pressure at low loadings, probably due to the simple experimental set-up that was used for the measurements at low CO2 partial pressure.

texts.orig: In Section 4.1, only results from experiments at an F -factor of 1.7 Pa 0.5 (reasonably high fluid dynamic load for the packing) were presented. In the experiments discussed in the following, the flue gas flow rate ( F -factor) is reduced at a fixed L / G ratio in order to study the influence of overall mass transfer kinetics (Mangalapally and Hasse, 2011b; Mangalapally et al., 2012). The main operating conditions for the variation of the flue gas flow rate are summarized in Table 4. The L / G ratio was kept at the optimum L / G ratio for MEA (2.5 kg/kg) for both Solvent A and B. For Solvent B, additionally experiments were carried out at its own optimum L / G ratio (1.4 kg/kg).

For Solvent A and MEA, this decrease in the specific reboiler duty /Delta1 q (upon decreasing the F -factor from 1.7 Pa 0.5 to 0.9 Pa 0.5 ) is in the order of 0.45 GJ/tCO 2 , indicating similar overall mass transfer kinetics. For Solvent B, /Delta1 q is 0.7 GJ/tCO 2 and, hence, slightly higher, indicating slightly slower overall mass transfer kinetics. This is in line with the larger distance between operating line and equilibrium curve for Solvent B compared to MEA, cf. Fig. 4. For Solvent B, the variation of the flue gas flow rate was also carried out at its own optimum L / G ratio (1.4 kg/kg). At this L / G ratio, the decrease of the reboiler duty /Delta1 q opt is 0.5 GJ/tCO 2 and, hence, lower than /Delta1 q at an L / G ratio of 2.5 kg/kg (0.7 GJ/tCO 2 ). Thus, for Solvent B, the decrease of the reboiler duty depends slightly on the L / G ratio. For CESAR2, this dependency was much lower, see Mangalapally and Hasse (2011b).

texts.orig: Fig. 6(b) shows the rich and lean CO2-loadings for the flue gas flow rate variation. For the experiments carried out at an L / G ratio of 2.5 kg/kg, the CO2 rich and lean loadings increase with decreasing F -factor for all solvents. The difference between the CO2-loading of the rich and the lean solvent is constant, since the CO2 removal rate and the L / G ratio are kept constant (Mangalapally and Hasse, 2011b). For Solvent B, the loadings observed at F -factors of 1.3 Pa 0.5 and 1.7 Pa 0.5 are similar because the amine concentration at an F -factor of 1.3 Pa 0.5 was significantly lower than the target amine concentration ( -0.025 g/g for AMP and -0.01 g/g for EDA). For the experiments with Solvent B at an L / G ratio of 1.4 kg/kg, the rich and lean CO2-loadings also increase with decreasing F -factor, but the increase is not as large as for the experiments with an L / G ratio of 2.5 kg/kg because equilibrium is approached for the low F -factor, see also the operating lines in Fig. 7(c) and (d).

texts.orig: Fig. 7 shows the absorber operating lines and equilibrium curves at 40 ◦ C (when available) for MEA, Solvent A, and Solvent B for the flue gas flow rate variation. As the L / G ratio is kept constant, the slope of the operating lines remains constant and only the CO2loadings change. At lower F -factors, less driving force is needed as less CO2 needs to be transferred per interfacial area. Thus, the distance between operating lines and equilibrium curves is smaller and the operating lines shift towards the equilibrium curves. This behavior is observed for all solvents.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.3. Estimation of reboiler duty in higher columns
texts.text: 4.3. Estimation of reboiler duty in higher columns

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The absolute numbers for the optimum L / G ratio and the reboiler duty depend on the design of the pilot plant, especially if kinetic limitations are present. The specific reboiler duty at the lowest F -factor tested in the present work (0.9 Pa 0.5 ) is more representative of the reboiler duty in higher columns (without mass transfer limitation) as more interfacial area is available per amount of CO2 transferred. Fig. 8 shows estimates for the specific reboiler duty as a function of the L / G ratio at an F -factor of 0.9 Pa 0.5 . The estimates are obtained by subtraction of /Delta1 q obtained in the gas flow variation, cf. Section 4.2.1 and Mangalapally and Hasse (2011b), from the specific reboiler duty at an F -factor of 1.7 Pa 0.5 , cf. Fig. 1. Note that /Delta1 q is obtained at the optimum L / G ratio for MEA (2.5 kg/kg) and assumed to be independent of the L / G ratio. The specific reboiler duty is significantly reduced for all solvents and compared to Fig. 1, the difference in the specific reboiler duty between the solvents becomes smaller. The optima are shown in Fig. 9, both as absolute numbers (a) and in a normalized format (b). According to this estimation, Solvent A, Solvent B and CESAR1 would all perform similarly well in a higher column (improvement of approximately 18% in the reboiler duty compared to MEA). In Fig. 9, however, also the estimates for the reboiler duty when subtracting /Delta1 q opt instead of /Delta1 q are shown. While there is no significant influence for CESAR2, the relative improvement for Solvent B compared to MEA drops from 17% to 12%.

The optima are shown in Fig. 9, both as absolute numbers (a) and in a normalized format (b). According to this estimation, Solvent A, Solvent B and CESAR1 would all perform similarly well in a higher column (improvement of approximately 18% in the reboiler duty compared to MEA). In Fig. 9, however, also the estimates for the reboiler duty when subtracting /Delta1 q opt instead of /Delta1 q are shown. While there is no significant influence for CESAR2, the relative improvement for Solvent B compared to MEA drops from 17% to 12%.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 5. Conclusions
texts.text: 5. Conclusions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Two new aqueous amine solvents for PCC (Solvent A and Solvent B) are tested in a pilot plant using a consistent procedure and compared to data for the standard solvent MEA and the solvents CESAR1 and CESAR2 from a previous study in the same pilot plant using the same methodology. The CO2 partial pressure in the flue gas is always 100 mbar and the CO2 removal rate 90%. For each solvent, two different parameter studies are carried out. In the first parameter study, the solvent flow rate and consequently the L / G ratio is varied. From the results, the optimum L / G ratio and the corresponding minimum reboiler duty is determined. For Solvent A and B, the minimum reboiler duty is reduced to 3.4 GJ/tCO 2 at an L / G ratio of 1.6 kg/kg and to 3.7 GJ/tCO 2 at an L / G ratio of 1.4 kg/kg, respectively, compared to 4.1 GJ/tCO 2 for MEA at an L / G ratio of 2.5 kg/kg.
texts.text: Two new aqueous amine solvents for PCC (Solvent A and Solvent B) are tested in a pilot plant using a consistent procedure and compared to data for the standard solvent MEA and the solvents CESAR1 and CESAR2 from a previous study in the same pilot plant using the same methodology. The CO2 partial pressure in the flue gas is always 100 mbar and the CO2 removal rate 90%. For each solvent, two different parameter studies are carried out. In the first parameter study, the solvent flow rate and consequently the L / G ratio is varied. From the results, the optimum L / G ratio and the corresponding minimum reboiler duty is determined. For Solvent A and B, the minimum reboiler duty is reduced to 3.4 GJ/tCO 2 at an L / G ratio of 1.6 kg/kg and to 3.7 GJ/tCO 2 at an L / G ratio of 1.4 kg/kg, respectively, compared to 4.1 GJ/tCO 2 for MEA at an L / G ratio of 2.5 kg/kg.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The absolute numbers for the optimum L / G ratio and minimum reboiler duty depend both on the operating conditions (e.g. partial pressure of CO2 in flue gas and the CO2 removal rate) and on the design of the pilot plant (Mangalapally and Hasse, 2011b). The pilot plant used in the present work has a small packed height of the columns compared to industrial plants and therefore kinetic effects and heat losses play a larger role. Therefore, only a relative comparison between the solvents in terms of reboiler duty and mass transfer kinetics is possible. In the second parameter study, the influence of overall mass transfer kinetics on the results is studied by lowering the flue gas flow rate at constant L / G ratio. The results indicate slightly higher overall mass transfer kinetics for Solvent B and similar overall mass transfer kinetics for Solvent A and MEA. Hence, the relative improvement in the reboiler duty compared to MEA for Solvent A (16%) is expected to be transferable to larger installations. For Solvent B, the relative improvement in the reboiler duty (9%) is expected to be slightly larger for higher columns (approximately 12%).

For higher L / G ratios, the liquid phase temperature is higher along the absorber height for both solvents while at the same time the outlet gas temperature is lower. Most of the CO2 absorption takes place in the lower part of the absorber column at high L / G ratios, leading to high liquid temperatures in the absorber bottom. For low L / G ratios, most of the CO2 absorption takes place in the upper part of the absorber column, leading to high outlet gas temperatures and low outlet liquid temperatures (Mangalapally and Hasse, 2011a). For both Solvent A and Solvent B (and MEA, cf. Mangalapally and Hasse, 2011a), the maximum temperatures achieved at the same L / G ratios are similar and in the range of 69-75 ◦ C. For Solvent A, the temperature maximum and also the outlet gas temperatures (after the absorber and before the washing section) are slightly lower, indicating a slightly lower enthalpy of absorption compared to Solvent B. For Solvent B, the enthalpy of absorption is possibly slightly higher than that of MEA. Nevertheless, the enthalpy of absorption is always within the same range for all tested amine solutions.

texts.orig: Solvent B, at high L / G ratios the change in CO2 concentration is larger at the absorber bottom. Hence, most of the CO2 is absorbed in the lower part of the absorber (Mangalapally and Hasse, 2011a). Therefore, higher temperatures are reached in the absorber bottom (cf. Fig. A.1), resulting in lower equilibrium CO2-loadings as the gas solubility decreases with increasing temperature. The measured rich loadings are correspondingly lower for higher L / G ratios, although here also kinetic effects play a role. For low L / G ratios, the change in CO2 concentration is larger towards the absorber top which means that most of the CO2 is absorbed in the upper part of the absorber. Therefore, the temperatures in the absorber bottom are lower, resulting in higher equilibrium CO2-loadings. In a plot of the transferred CO2 mass flow over the column height as shown in Fig. A.3, the parts of the absorber column which are most important for the CO2 mass transfer can be observed more easily. The transferred CO2 mass flow in the column is calculated from the CO2 concentration profile and the assumption of a constant liquid flow rate in the absorber. From this graph, it is clear that CO2 absorption predominantly takes places in the upper part of the column for low L / G ratios while it predominantly takes place in the lower part of the column for high L / G ratios.

texts.orig: The desorber temperature profiles for different L / G ratios are shown in Fig. A.4. For high L / G ratios, the temperature decreases most rapidly in the lower part of the desorber, indicating that most of the CO2 is desorbed there (Mangalapally and Hasse, 2011a). Close to the optimum L / G ratio (1.6 kg/kg and 1.4 kg/kg for Solvent A and Solvent B, respectively), the temperature decrease is linear over the height of the desorber column, indicating an optimal use of the desorber (Mangalapally and Hasse, 2011b). For Solvent B, the maximum temperature decrease is already shifted towards the column top at L / G = 1.4 kg/kg. At a slightly higher L / G ratio for Solvent B of L / G = 1.6 kg/kg, the temperature decrease is however distributed throughout the desorber column (not shown). As it can be seen in Fig. 1, the reboiler duty is almost similar at L / G = 1.4 kg/kg and L / G = 1.6 kg/kg. At L / G ratios lower than the optimum (not shown here), the temperature decreases most rapidly in the upper part of the desorber, indicating that most of the CO2 is desorbed there.
